headline: Washington's Defense Stifles Miami Despite Mertz's Heroic Passing Display
body: Graham Mertz put on a clinic through the air with 389 passing yards and three touchdowns, earning Player of the Game honors, yet Miami still found itself on the wrong side of a 35-28 final. The Miami quarterback was surgical in his decision-making, completing 30 of 53 attempts while his receivers, particularly Campen with eight receptions for 89 yards, gave him reliable targets downfield. But a single turnover and an anemic rushing attack that managed just 35 yards told the real story of Miami's afternoon.

Washington's path to victory ran through suffocating defense and ball security. The Huskies' ground game, led by their namesake running back with 62 yards on 11 carries, may not have been explosive, but it was efficient enough to control tempo. More importantly, Washington committed zero turnovers while forcing Miami into one critical mistake. Defensive end Clemons led the way with 12 tackles and a sack, systematizing pressure that forced Mertz into difficult situations despite his prolific output.

The fundamental difference emerged in how each team moved the ball on the ground. Miami's inability to establish any running game left them one-dimensional and predictable, forcing Mertz to beat Washington through the air alone. Washington, meanwhile, stayed balanced and methodical, rushing for 93 yards as a team. That asymmetry in execution, more than raw yardage totals, proved decisive in a game where Miami's passing prowess could not overcome a structural offensive imbalance.
turningPoint: Washington's defense forcing Miami's fumble while maintaining zero turnovers of their own created a possession swing that a one-dimensional Miami offense could not overcome in the final minutes.

unsungHero.name: Clemons
unsungHero.why: His 12 tackles and disruptive sack set the tone for Washington's defensive effort and forced Mertz into uncomfortable decisions, yet the POG went to Miami's quarterback despite the loss
